fabricated_divergences.quote: German-language coverage (Tagesschau, DW) foregrounds government officials' statements and structural/economic analysis (police costs, youth unemployment, historical parallels to Ghana and Uganda expulsions), while French-language coverage (RFI, France 24) foregrounds affected migrants' visceral fear and testimony, such as a pastor saying refugees no longer fear death and a taxi driver describing constant unease.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: The historical parallels to Ghana and Uganda expulsions appear only in article 8 (AllAfrica, US, en), not in any German coverage; DW (article 3) is English-language, and the structural/unemployment analysis comes from DW, not Tagesschau (article 6), which quotes only officials and the movement founder. France 24 (article 4) is English-language, not French, and contains no substantive migrant testimony.

fabricated_divergences.quote: German-language sources (Tagesschau) give prominent voice to March and March founder Jacinta Ngobese-Zuma rejecting xenophobia labels and demanding government solutions to illegal immigration, whereas English-language sources from the UK (BBC) and the US (AllAfrica) frame the movement more critically, emphasizing its association with violence, mob killings, and lawlessness.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: The framing emphasizing mob killings and lawlessness is present only in article 1 (AllAfrica, US); BBC (article 2) does not emphasize violence or mob killings — its analysis reports Ramaphosa's constitutional-protection warning and individual migrant departure accounts.

fabricated_divergences.quote: European outlets (DW, Tagesschau, RFI) quote or feature civil society and academic analysts (Tessa Dooms, Fredson Guilengue, Vanya Gastrow) who contextualize the crisis within governance failures, whereas Qatar-based Al Jazeera centers ground-level reporting from informal settlements and markets, prioritizing affected-community testimony over expert analysis.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: Vanya Gastrow is quoted in article 8 (AllAfrica, US), not in any European outlet; and Al Jazeera (article 7) quotes academic Luke Sinwell of the University of Johannesburg, directly contradicting the claim that Al Jazeera prioritizes affected-community testimony over expert analysis.
